title: Quantum state isomorphism problems for groups
authors: Alexandru Gheorghiu, Dale Jacobs, Saeed Mehraban, Arsalan Motamedi
abstract: We study the computational complexity of quantum state isomorphism problems under group actions: given two quantum circuits that prepare pure or mixed states, decide whether the two states are related by a group action. This can be seen as a quantum state version of the Hidden Shift Problem, in much the same way that the State Hidden Subgroup Problem is a quantum version of the ordinary Hidden Subgroup Problem.   We prove several results for this computational problem:   - For the pure-state version, we show that the problem is BQP-hard for all nontrivial groups, and contained in QCMA $\cap$ QCSZK. We further obtain refined results for specific groups of interest: for abelian groups we show that the problem reduces to the state hidden subgroup problem over the generalized dihedral group; for the Clifford group, the problem is at least as hard as Graph Isomorphism under polynomial-time reductions; for the Pauli group it is BQP-complete.   - For the mixed-state version, for nontrivial, finite and efficiently representable groups, the problem is QSZK-complete.   - We also study a variant of this problem over an infinite group, in particular, the bosonic linear optical unitaries. We show that in the setting where the classical description of the quantum state is given in a suitable wave function representation known as the stellar representation, the problem is at least as hard as Graph Isomorphism, and is contained in NP $\cap$ SZK.   Prior to our work, state isomorphism problems had only been studied for the symmetric group [LG17]. As a consequence of our results, we resolve an open question posed in [HEC25] about the existence of a quantum algorithm for the abelian state hidden subgroup problem on mixed states. We show that this problem is QSZK-hard in the worst case, thereby ruling out an efficient quantum algorithm unless QSZK = BQP.
summary: This paper provides a comprehensive complexity classification for determining if two quantum states are equivalent under group actions. It reveals that while some cases like the Pauli group are efficiently solvable, others are as hard as the Graph Isomorphism problem or even QSZK-complete, ruling out efficient quantum algorithms for certain mixed-state problems.
main_problem: The paper investigates the computational complexity of Quantum State Isomorphism Problems (QSIP) under the action of various groups, determining if two quantum states are related by a group element.
main_result: The authors prove that pure-state QSIP is BQP-hard for all nontrivial groups, BQP-complete for the Pauli group, and GI-hard for the Clifford group, while mixed-state QSIP is QSZK-complete.
method: The study employs complexity theory reductions (from Graph Isomorphism, Hidden Subgroup Problem, and Quantum State Distinguishability), classical shadows, and stabilizer-rank analysis.
model_or_system: The models include n-qubit systems (pure and mixed states), the Pauli and Clifford groups, and infinite-dimensional bosonic systems using the stellar representation and linear optical unitaries.
key_observables: Fidelity, trace distance, and inner products between quantum states.
important_parameters: Precision parameters (alpha, beta) for state closeness, photon number (r) for bosonic states, and the trace condition (mu) for group representations.
main_assumptions: Assumes efficient representability of groups, finite-dimensional or succinctly represented bosonic states, and an unbounded prover in the complexity protocols.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the QSIP framework, analyzes pure-state complexity for various group families (Pauli, Clifford, Abelian), extends the analysis to mixed states, investigates bosonic/linear optical systems, and concludes with implications for the State Hidden Subgroup Problem.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum information as it establishes the fundamental computational limits of identifying symmetries and equivalence classes in both discrete-variable and continuous-variable quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
